document_metadata.title: Micro-Copy Design in Health & Performance Apps
document_metadata.subtitle: Evidence Review: Linguistic Authority, Narrative vs. Data Framing, Personalization, Behavioral Specificity, and Notification Architecture

executive_summary.text: The most effective health micro-copy combines narrative framing with anchored data, is written in active voice at a 6th-8th grade reading level, is personalized to the individual's own baseline rather than population norms, and encodes specific if-then behavioral prompts rather than generic suggestions. For YU -- a platform delivering individualized SPC-based biometric intelligence -- these principles translate directly into a defensible copy architecture that drives both trust and action.

rows.key_finding: Narrative + data hybrid outperforms raw data
4x open rate lift; 18% behavior change increase
d=0.65 vs goal intent; +13% relative compliance

section_1_authority_and_trust.section_subtitle: What Makes Copy Feel Like a Clinician, Not an App
section_1_authority_and_trust.intro_text: Trust in health copy is not primarily about content -- it is about linguistic signals. Three bodies of evidence converge on a consistent architecture.

neutrality_over_enthusiasm.finding: A study of 242 users in online health forums found that authors using positive adjectives like "outstanding" and "excellent" were rated less trustworthy, with lower perceived goodwill, integrity, and credibility than authors who used neutral language. Superlatives trigger skepticism heuristics. Users infer manipulative intent.
neutrality_over_enthusiasm.rule: Remove "Great job!", "Amazing!", and "Outstanding" from every health notification. Replace with neutral observation. Enthusiasm is the enemy of authority in health contexts.

precision_as_trust_signal.finding: Linguistic research on health rumor detection (Frontiers in Public Health, 2024) identifies that credible health claims use fewer extreme adverbs and promotional language, but more specific numerical references.
precision_as_trust_signal.rule: Specificity reads as measurement. Vagueness reads as manipulation.

pairs.high_trust: "Sleep efficiency: 84%. On track for your recovery."

pairs.low_trust: "You might want to consider a break"
pairs.high_trust: "Your nervous system needs deload. One hard session max today."

confident_not_hedging.finding: Linguist Deborah Tannen's research shows indirect, hedged language is associated with distrust and is perceived as evasive. Researchers Fox Tree & Liu found that hedged information ("the shirt cost, like, $15") is flagged by listeners as inaccurate or unimportant. The credible voice says: "Your recovery is low. Your body needs more than training today." Not: "It seems like maybe you might be a little tired."

psd_credibility_framework.description: The Persuasive Systems Design framework identifies four credibility support mechanisms in high-efficacy health apps (based on 92 RCTs, 16,728 participants):
mechanisms: Trustworthiness -- fair and objective information (present in 80% of top-efficacy apps)
Expertise -- demonstrated domain knowledge and competence (only 4% of current apps)
Surface credibility -- professional design, tone, and visual cues
Third-party endorsements -- clinical frameworks, cited protocols
psd_credibility_framework.yu_implication: The "Expertise" gap is a major white-space opportunity. Most apps claim trustworthiness but demonstrate no expertise. Copy that references your SPC methodology ("based on your personal 30-day baseline, not population averages") fills this gap directly.

section_2_language_format_and_reading_le.section_title: Language Format, Reading Level & Sentence Structure

reading_level_evidence.subsection_title: Reading Level: What the Evidence Says
reading_level_evidence.ama_recommendation: The American Medical Association recommends a 6th-grade reading level for all patient-facing health materials.
reading_level_evidence.heart_failure_apps_finding: A review of 10 heart failure mHealth apps found an average reading level of 9.35 grade, with only 1 of 10 meeting the threshold.
reading_level_evidence.app_store_finding: A separate analysis of app store descriptions found an average of 11th-15th grade, with only 6% meeting recommendations.
reading_level_evidence.rationale_for_high_performers: For high-performing professionals, the 6th-grade standard is not about treating users as low-literacy. It is about cognitive efficiency. High-achievers are reading your notification in two seconds, in context, under load. Clarity is a performance feature, not a dumbing-down.

acm_chi_2024_study.subsection_title: Narrative vs. Data-Forward Language: The Research Verdict
acm_chi_2024_study.study_description: A 2024 ACM CHI study (n=273) tested three conditions for presenting fitness data: text/narrative only, chart only (data-forward), and chart + text (hybrid).
acm_chi_2024_study.table_caption: ACM CHI 2024 results -- text vs. chart conditions (n=273)
acm_chi_2024_study.meta_analysis_finding: Statistical evidence drives stronger impact on beliefs and attitudes (cognitive); narrative evidence drives stronger impact on behavioral intentions (motivational). Use narrative to drive action. Anchor with data to maintain credibility.

optimal_copy_formula.example: "Your body didn't fully recover last night. HRV: 38ms -- 22% below your average. Your nervous system is still adapting. Train at 70% today."
optimal_copy_formula.table_caption: Anatomy of an optimal biometric reading

section_3_personalization.section_title: Personalization: Effects on Value & Action Rates

quantitative_evidence.behavior_triggered_finding: An analysis of 1.5 billion push notifications found that behavior-triggered delivery -- sending notifications in response to a specific detected action or anomaly -- achieves an 8.0% open rate versus 1.5% for generic scheduled notifications. That is an 800% differential. For YU: a notification triggered by a 2sigma SPC anomaly is categorically more effective than a daily 7am push.

push_notification_open_rates.table_caption: Push notification open rates by personalization type (Leanplum, 1.5B notifications)

section_3_personalization.rct_findings: A randomized controlled trial found that patients receiving individually tailored behavioral feedback were 18% more likely to change at least one risk behavior than those receiving generic feedback (OR=1.18, 95% CI 1.00-1.39). The tailored messaging drove statistically significant changes in physical activity, dietary fat, and cholesterol screening. Mechanism: the Elaboration Likelihood Model -- tailored messages route through the central (high-effort, durable) processing path rather than the peripheral path.
section_3_personalization.jmir_experiment: A JMIR mHealth experiment (n=262) found that self-efficacy, health consciousness, health motivation, and extraversion together explained 40.3% of variance in perceived persuasiveness of mHealth copy. One-size-fits-all copy leaves 40% of persuasion potential on the table.

individual_vs_population_baseline.subsection_title: Individual Baseline vs. Population Norm Personalization
individual_vs_population_baseline.principle: The most powerful form of personalization in biometric contexts is anchoring to the user's own historical data not to population comparisons.
individual_vs_population_baseline.example_comparison: "You are 2.2sigma below your personal HRV baseline for the third consecutive day" is more salient, more scientifically accurate, and more trusted than "your HRV is 30% below average users." The former is YU's core copy defense. The latter is every competitor's approach.

section_4_specificity_in_behavioral_prom.section_title: Specificity in Behavioral Prompts: Compliance Rates

implementation_intentions_literature.description: The most robust body of evidence on prompt specificity comes from Gollwitzer's implementation intentions research -- 94 studies, replicated across health, exercise, and clinical contexts. The core principle: if-then plans that specify when, where, and how to act produce dramatically larger behavior change than mere goal intentions.

rows.finding: Initiating goal behavior vs. goal intention alone
Overall goal attainment vs. mere goal intention
Date+time prompt vs. control (date only non-sig)

section_4_specificity_in_behavioral_prom.vaccination_experiment: The vaccination field experiment (PNAS, 2011) demonstrates the marginal effect of adding a single specificity element. "Write down the date AND time" vs. "write down the date" -- one additional field -- produced a statistically significant +13% relative increase in compliance (37.1% vs 35.6%, p=0.04). The mechanism: specific situational cues become automatically accessible, requiring no in-the-moment deliberation.

vague_vs_specific_prompts.table_caption: Vague vs. specific behavioral prompts and expected compliance delta
vague_vs_specific_prompts.principle: Effective if-then plans must spell out an unambiguous opportunity (the when/where) and a specific response (the exactly what). "Exercise more tomorrow" is equivalent to a mere goal intention -- ineffective. "If it is 6am tomorrow, then I will do 20 minutes of mobility" is an implementation intention.

pairs.specific: "Drink 500ml of water before you open your laptop"
"After your meeting ends, do 5 minutes of slow breathing"

section_4_specificity_in_behavioral_prom.biometric_app_implication: For behavior-triggered notifications where the cue is an app-detected anomaly, the situational cue is already embedded in the delivery timing. The copy needs only to supply the then-part: what exactly to do, when exactly, and for how long. This is where most health apps systematically underperform -- they deliver the detection but leave the action undefined.

section_5_notification_architecture.section_title: Notification Architecture: Trusted Advisor vs. App Alert

seven_rules.subsection_title: The Seven Rules for Trusted Advisor Copy

rules.title: Lead with observation, not command
rules.description: Empathy-centered UX research establishes that high-trust digital health experiences validate before instructing. "Your body didn't fully recover last night" precedes "here's what to do about it" -- never the reverse.

rules.title: State the mechanism briefly
rules.description: Digital empathy research requires contextualizing and personalizing communication. "HRV suppressed by 22% -> nervous system still in recovery mode" is a consultation. "HRV low" is an alert. The why transforms one into the other.

rules.title: Never exceed the user's cognitive budget
rules.description: Notifications increased app open probability 3.5x within one hour. The first 25 characters carry the full weight. If a user receives a notification during high-focus work, a long notification is dismissed without reading.

rules.title: One insight, one action
rules.description: The PSD tunnelling principle (most consistently supported in digital health RCTs) establishes that notifications with multiple data points, multiple recommendations, or branching options trigger choice paralysis. The advisor says one thing. The app says everything.

rules.title: Gain-frame prevention; Loss-frame detection
rules.description: Rothman-Salovey Prospect Theory applied to health: gain-framed messages are more effective for low-risk prevention behaviors (walk, sleep, hydrate). Loss-framed messages are more effective for detection behaviors (check your HRV, complete a check-in, review a reading).

rules.title: Behavior-triggered delivery is 800x more effective than scheduled
rules.description: Leanplum analysis of 1.5B notifications: behavior-triggered notifications achieve 8.0% open rate vs. 1.5% for generic scheduled. For YU, a notification triggered by a 2sigma SPC anomaly is an order of magnitude more likely to be opened than a daily 7am push.

rules.title: Never use positive adjectives or exclamation points
rules.description: The health forum credibility study is definitive: "outstanding," "great job," and promotional enthusiasm actively lower trust and credibility ratings. For high-performing professionals, congratulatory copy reads as patronizing. Remove it entirely.

layers.example: "Before you train: 5 min box breathing. Body responds better."

section_6_yu_microcopy_framework.intro_text: Drawing all five dimensions together, the following rules constitute a defensible micro-copy architecture for YU's biometric agent.

seven_rules_table.table_caption: The seven YU micro-copy rules with evidence anchoring

rules.rule: Lead with narrative, anchor with data
Personalize to individual SPC baseline, not population norms
Encode if-then specificity in every behavioral prompt
Gain-frame prevention prompts; loss-frame detection prompts
One insight, one action per notification

copy_mode_selection_matrix.table_caption: Copy mode selection matrix for YU agent states

rows.example: "Your body is processing yesterday. Movement today will speed recovery."
"Three suppressed HRV days. This is the window to intervene."
"Solid recovery. HRV 62ms -- right in your zone."
"After you close your laptop, take a 10-minute walk before dinner."

patterns.fix: "Try this: 5 min slow breathing before bed"
